title: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis): A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
Identify key diagnostic features of Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
Apply clinical reasoning to Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis) management
Recognize common pitfalls in Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)

sections.title: Overview of Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
sections.content:
**Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)**

Limited vs diffuse, CREST syndrome, anti-centromere vs anti-Scl-70, pulmonary complications

**Key Points:**
• Limited (CREST): Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia
• Limited: anti-centromere antibody; pulmonary arterial hypertension main cause of death
• Diffuse: anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I); rapid skin thickening, renal crisis, pulmonary fibrosis

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Limited (CREST): Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia
• Limited: anti-centromere antibody; pulmonary arterial hypertension main cause of death

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Diffuse: anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I); rapid skin thickening, renal crisis, pulmonary fibrosis
• Scleroderma renal crisis: malignant HTN + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia → treat with ACE inhibitors
• Raynaud phenomenon: digital vasospasm (white → blue → red); first manifestation in many patients

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis):**

• Diffuse: anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I); rapid skin thickening, renal crisis, pulmonary fibrosis
• Scleroderma renal crisis: malignant HTN + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia → treat with ACE inhibitors
• Raynaud phenomenon: digital vasospasm (white → blue → red); first manifestation in many patients

options.text: Limited (CREST): Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia
options.explanation: Limited (CREST): Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

options.text: Raynaud phenomenon: digital vasospasm (white → blue → red); first manifestation in many patients
options.explanation: Raynaud phenomenon: digital vasospasm (white → blue → red); first manifestation in many patients

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis). Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Limited: anti-centromere antibody; pulmonary arterial hypertension main cause of death
options.explanation: Limited: anti-centromere antibody; pulmonary arterial hypertension main cause of death

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.
